title: Strategy optimization for quantum conference key agreement in asymmetric star networks
authors: Janka Memmen, Julia Kunzelmann, Nathan Walk, Jens Eisert, Julius Wallnöfer
abstract: The distribution of entangled states is a core task for quantum networks facilitating quantum communication, and the use of multipartite entangled states comes with its own set of considerations. In this work, we analyze a quantum conference agreement protocol based on GHZ states in a network with a central station to which multiple clients are connected. Using comprehensive numerical simulations, we investigate how minor variations in the scenario-such as the number of parties, the number of memories, and asymmetric distances from the central station-can drastically influence the performance of the protocol. In particular, we demonstrate that it is crucial to adjust the strategy by optimizing cutoff times. From a broader perspective, we argue that numerical simulations are an indispensable tool for protocol design for devising realistic schemes for quantum communication.
summary: This paper investigates how to optimize quantum conference key agreement in asymmetric star networks by using cutoff times to discard decohered qubits. The authors demonstrate that such strategies, along with memory multiplexing, can significantly extend the achievable distance and improve the security of multipartite entanglement distribution.
main_problem: Optimizing strategies for Quantum Conference Key Agreement (CKA) in asymmetric star networks to mitigate the impact of memory dephasing and decoherence.
main_result: Implementing 'cutoff time' strategies (discarding qubits after a certain duration) significantly enhances state fidelity and can enable secure key generation in regimes where standard protocols fail.
method: Numerical Monte Carlo simulations using the NetSquid and ReQuSim frameworks to evaluate key rates and fidelity across various network configurations.
model_or_system: A star-shaped quantum network consisting of a central station and multiple clients, utilizing GHZ state distribution, quantum memories with dephasing noise, and asymmetric link distances.
key_observables: Secure key rate (K_CKA), Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), and GHZ state fidelity.
important_parameters: Number of parties (N), number of memories (m), cutoff time (t_cut), memory dephasing time (T_dp), and link distance (l).
main_assumptions: Dephasing noise is biased towards one basis; dark count rate and initial fidelity are fixed; the university network scenario is fictitious.
figures_summary: Figures show the improvement in fidelity and relative advantage in key rates using cutoff times as a function of dephasing time and distance, as well as the advantage of multipartite vs. bipartite protocols.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CKA problem in star networks, describes the physical model and noise parameters, presents numerical simulation results for symmetric and asymmetric scenarios, evaluates the impact of memory multiplexing and cutoff times, and compares multipartite vs. bipartite advantages.
why_interesting: The study addresses the management of decoherence in multi-node quantum networks, which is highly relevant to the development of scalable quantum communication architectures and open quantum system dynamics in networked environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Star network representing a symmetric single-memory multipartite quantum repeater. The N parties Bi are located around the central station C at equal distances l. The central quantum repeater provides a single quantum memory (QM) for each party.

Figure 2: Fidelities (a) and key rates (b) for the different strategies in dependence of a single link’s distance for a fully symmetric star network with N = 4 participants. The different markers symbolize whether a cutoff time of tcut = 0.3 s was used or not. Other parameters are chosen as: PLINK = 1, FINIT = 0.99, TP = 10−6 s, PD = 10−6, Tdp = 1 s, m = 1.

Figure 3: Fidelities (a) and key rates (b) for the different strategies in dependence of a single link’s distance l for a fully symmetric star network with N = 4 parties, when every party can make use of m = 5 quantum memories. The different markers symbolize whether a cutoff time of tcut = 0.1 s has been used or not. Other parameters are chosen as PLINK = 1, FINIT = 0.99, TP = 10−6 s, PD = 10−6, Tdp = 1 s.

Figure 4: Key rates in dependence of different cutoff times for a completely symmetric star network, when every parties possesses a single memory (round markers) or m = 5 memories (triangle markers). Other parameters are chosen as N = 4, l = 150 km, PLINK = 1, FINIT = 0.99, TP = 10−6 s, PD = 10−6, Tdp = 1 s.

Figure 5: Key rates in dependence of the applied cutoff time tcut and the distance for the scenario Dis-C (a) and Meas-B (b). Other parameters are chosen as: N = 4, PLINK = 1, FINIT = 0.99, TP = 10−6 s, PD = 10−6, Tdp = 1 s, m = 1.
